concepts.title: Linear Expansion of Solids
concepts.conceptBody:
Linear Expansion of Solids

Coefficient of linear expansion (α) : 

The fractional charge is length for $1\text{C}$ (or $1\text{ K}$) change in temperature is called coefficient of linear expansion $\left( \alpha \right)$.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \alpha =\left( \frac{\Delta\text{ l}}{\text{l }\Delta\theta } \right) \\ \Delta\text{ l}={{\text{l}}_{0}}\alpha \Delta\theta \\ \therefore  1-{{\text{l}}_{0}}={{\text{l}}_{0}}\alpha \Delta\theta \\ \text{l}={{\text{l}}_{0}}\left( l+\alpha \Delta\theta \right) \\ \text{ unit }\text{ of }\alpha :{{\text{C}}^{-1}}\text{ or }{{\text{K}}^{-1}} \\ \end{array}$

Absolute Value : 

$\alpha=\lim _{\Delta \theta \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{l} \Delta \theta}\right)$

$\therefore \quad \alpha=\left(\frac{\mathrm{dl}}{\mathrm{l}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}\right)$

$\int_{l_0}^1 \frac{\mathrm{dl}}{\mathrm{l}}=\int_{\theta_0}^\theta \alpha \mathrm{d} \theta$

$\Rightarrow \quad \ln \left(\frac{1}{l_0}\right)=\int_{\theta_0}^\theta \alpha \mathrm{d} \theta=\mathrm{a} \Delta \theta$ (if $\alpha$ is const.)

$\Rightarrow \quad \ln \left(\frac{1+\Delta \mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{l}_0}\right)=\alpha \Delta \theta$

$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{l}_0}\right)=\alpha \Delta \theta\left(\right.$ approx.$\left.\quad \therefore \ln (1+\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{x}^3}{3}-\frac{\mathrm{x}^4}{4}+\ldots\right)$

$\therefore \quad \alpha=\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{l}_0 \Delta \theta}\right)$ [Avg. value]

concepts.title: Area Expansion
concepts.conceptBody:
Area Expansion

Coefficient of Superficial Expansion (𝛃) : 

The fractional change in area for $1\text{C}$ (or $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}$ ) change in temperature is called coefficient of superficial expansion $\left( \beta \right)$.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \beta =\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ A}}{\text{A }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta }\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (avg}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ value) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & \text{A}={{\text{A}}_{0}}\left( 1+\beta \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \right) \\ \end{array}$

Absolute value : 

$\beta =\underset{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ A}}{\text{A }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta }=\left( \frac{\text{dA}}{\text{A}} \right)\left( \frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}\theta } \right)$

$\mathop{\int }_{{{\text{A}}_{0}}}^{\text{A}}\frac{\text{dA}}{\text{A}}=\mathop{\int }_{{{\theta }_{0}}}^{\theta }\beta \text{d}\theta $

concepts.title: Volume Expansion
concepts.conceptBody:
Volume Expansion

Coefficient of Volume Expansion (γ) : 

$\gamma =\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}}{\text{V }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta }\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ avg}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ value }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)$

The fractional change in volume for $1\text{C}$ (or $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}$ ) change in temperature is called coefficient of volume expansion.

$\text{V}={{\text{V}}_{0}}\left( 1+\gamma \theta \right)$

Absolute value : 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \gamma =\underset{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\left( \frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}}{\text{V }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta } \right)=\left( \frac{\text{dV}}{\text{V}} \right)\left( \frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}\theta } \right) \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & ~\mathop{\int }_{{{\text{V}}_{0}}}^{\text{V}}\frac{\text{dV}}{\text{V}}=\mathop{\int }_{{{\theta }_{0}}}^{\theta }\gamma \text{d}\theta \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Thermal Expansion of Bimetallic Strip
concepts.conceptBody:
Thermal Expansion of Bimetallic Strip

Let us consider a bimetallic strip of length ${{l}_{0}}$ and both metals having breadth $d$ joint together. The 2 metals have coefficient of thermal expansion ${{\alpha }_{1}}$ and ${{\alpha }_{2}}$ respectively $\left( {{\alpha }_{1}}>{{\alpha }_{2}} \right)$

Now, when the temperature of this strip is increased, then this strip has the characteristic property of bending due to unequal linear expansion of the two metals.

Bending will take place in such a manner that the one with higher coefficient $\left( \alpha \right)$ will be on convex side.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{l}_{1}}={{l}_{0}}\left( 1+{{\alpha }_{1}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \right) \\ {} & {{l}_{2}}={{l}_{0}}\left( 1+{{\alpha }_{2}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \right) \\ \end{array}$

Since both subtend same angle at the centre,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{{{\text{l}}_{1}}}{\left( \text{R}+\frac{\text{d}}{2} \right)}=\frac{{{\text{l}}_{2}}}{\left( \text{R}-\frac{\text{d}}{2} \right)} \\ {} & \frac{\left( 1+{{\alpha }_{1}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \right)}{\left( 1+{{\alpha }_{2}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \right)}=\frac{R+d/2}{R-d/2} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{2+\overline{\left( {{\alpha }_{1}}+{{\alpha }_{2}} \right)}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta }{\left( {{\alpha }_{1}}-{{\alpha }_{2}} \right)\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta }=\left( \frac{2\text{R}}{\text{d}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ neglected }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & ~=\frac{2}{\left( {{\alpha }_{1}}-{{\alpha }_{2}} \right)\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta }=\frac{2R}{d} \\ {} & \text{R}=\frac{\text{d}}{\left( {{\alpha }_{1}}-{{\alpha }_{2}} \right)\theta } \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Thermal Stress
concepts.conceptBody:
Thermal Stress 

  • Due increase in temp., the rod tries to expand against the wall, but is prevented and the compressive stress hence developed is called thermal stress.
  • Similarly, due to decrease in temperature, tensile thermal stress develop which prevent the rod to contract.

Reminder : Stiffness corresponding to a rigid body $K = F/\Delta l$

$\therefore \mathrm{F}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{YA}}{\mathrm{l}}\right) \Delta \mathrm{l}$ 

Hence, you can treat it as spring with $\mathrm{K}$.

Expansion in length due to heating $=\left( l\alpha \Delta\theta \right)$

Due to stress developed contraction produced $\Delta\text{ l}=\left( \frac{\text{Fl}}{\text{YA}} \right)$

But the net expansion is zero. Hence,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \therefore  \left( \alpha \text{l }\Delta\theta \right)+\left( -\frac{\text{Fl}}{\text{YA}} \right)=0 \\ {} \frac{\text{Fl}}{\text{YA}}=\alpha \text{l }\Delta\theta \\ \therefore  \text{F}=\text{AY}\alpha \Delta\theta \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore $ Thermal Stress $=\left( \frac{\text{F}}{\text{A}} \right)=\alpha \text{Y }\Delta\theta $

concepts.title: Thermal Expansion in Liquids
concepts.conceptBody:
Thermal Expansion in Liquids 

${{\text{V}}_{\text{L}}}={{\text{V}}_{0}}\left( 1+{{\gamma }_{\text{L}}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \right)$

${{\text{V}}_{\text{S}}}={{\text{V}}_{0}}\left( 1+{{\gamma }_{\text{S}}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta \right)$

Change in volume, $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}={{\text{V}}_{\text{L}}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{S}}}$

$=\left( {{\gamma }_{\text{L}}}-{{\gamma }_{\text{S}}} \right){{\text{V}}_{0}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta $

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}=(\gamma $ apparent $){{\text{V}}_{0}}\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }\theta $

$\gamma $ apparent $={{\gamma }_{\text{L}}}-{{\gamma }_{\text{S}}}$

(1) If ${{\gamma }_{L}}>{{\gamma }_{s}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}>0$ (liquid level will rise)

(2) If ${{\gamma }_{\text{L}}}<{{\gamma }_{\text{S}}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}<0$ (liquid level will fall)

(3) If ${{\gamma }_{L}}={{\gamma }_{s}}$

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}=0[$ No change in liquid level]

concepts.title: Effect of Temperature on Density
concepts.conceptBody:
Effect of Temperature on Density

${{\rho }_{0}}=\frac{\text{m}}{{{\text{V}}_{0}}},\rho =\frac{\text{m}}{\text{V}}=\frac{\text{m}}{{{\text{V}}_{0}}\left( 1+\gamma \theta \right)}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\rho }{{{\rho }_{0}}}=\frac{1}{1+\gamma \theta }$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\rho =\frac{{{\rho }_{0}}}{1+\gamma \theta }$

Further,

\(\begin{array}{l}\rho=\rho_0(1+\gamma \theta)^{-1}=\rho_0(1-\gamma \theta) \\ \rho=\rho_0(1-\gamma \theta) \text { [This is an approx result] }\end{array}\)

Note : Also, sometimes in some questions, you do not get correct answer by just applying the above eqn. for a given temp. range.

In that case, start for initial condition at $\text{T}=0\text{C}$

e.g. Correlate ${{\rho }_{20}}$ and ${{\rho }_{70}}$

\(\rho_{70}=\rho_{20}(1-\gamma 50) \rightarrow\) This may not yield correct

In that case use,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {{\rho }_{20}} & ~=\rho _{0}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}\left( 1+\gamma 20 \right) \\ {{\rho }_{70}} & ~={{\rho }_{0}}\left( 1+\gamma 70 \right) \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{{{\rho }_{70}}}{{{\rho }_{20}}} & ~=\frac{\left( 1+70\gamma \right)}{\left( 1+20\gamma \right)} \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Effect of Temperature on Pendulum Clocks
concepts.conceptBody:
Effect of Temperature on Pendulum Clocks

If a pendulum clock keeps proper time at temperature $\theta$, at temperature ${\theta }'(>\theta )$ due to linear expansion, length of pendulum and hence time period will increase. Due to this increase in time period, in a given time interval (say $t$ ), the pendulum will exclude $\left( t/T \right)$ oscillations instead of ( $t$ / $\left. {\text{T'}} \right)$ and so will lose $\left[ \left( \text{t}/\text{T} \right)-\left( \text{t}/\text{T'} \right) \right]$ oscillations. Now, as measurement of time is through the counting of oscillations, so in time $t$, a clock with time $\text{T'}$ will lose time by

$\Delta\text{ t}=\text{T'}\left[ \frac{\text{t}}{\text{T}}-\frac{\text{t}}{{\text{T'}}} \right]=\text{t}\left[ \frac{{\text{T'}}}{\text{T}}-1 \right]$

also

$\text{T}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{{{\text{l}}_{0}}}{\text{ g}}},{{\text{T}}^{'}}=2\pi \sqrt{\frac{{{\text{l}}_{0}}\left( 1+\alpha \theta \right)}{\text{g}}}$

$\therefore \frac{{{\text{T}}^{\prime}}}{\text{ T}}=\sqrt{\left( 1+\alpha \Delta\theta \right)}$

$\therefore \Delta\text{ t}=\text{t}\left( \sqrt{1+\alpha \Delta\theta }-1 \right)$

Using Binomial expansion

\(\begin{array}{ll} & \Delta \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}\left(1+\frac{1}{2} \alpha \Delta \theta-1\right)=\frac{1}{2} \alpha \mathrm{t} \Delta \theta \\ & \therefore \frac{\Delta t}{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{1}{2} \alpha \theta \\ & \text { of factional loss/gain in time }=\frac{1}{2} \alpha \theta\end{array}\)

concepts.title: Expansion in Cavity
concepts.conceptBody:
Expansion in Cavity

If there is a hollow cavity (in case of solids) or hole (in case of lamina), then volume of the cavity (or hole) will increase, just like if it were supposed to be filled with the same material. 
 

\(\begin{gathered}\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{V}_0(1+\gamma \Delta \theta) \rightarrow \text { in case of cavity } \\ \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{A}_0(1+\beta \Delta \theta) \rightarrow \text { in case of hole }\end{gathered}\)

The volume of a gas at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $100 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ at normal pressure. If it is heated to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, its volume becomes 125 $\mathrm{cm}^3$ at the same pressure, then volume coefficient of the gas at normal pressure is

A uniform metal rod is used as a bar pendulum. If the room temperature rises by $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal of the rod is $2 \times 10^{-6}$ per ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the period of the pendulum will have percentage increase of
